items.q: Does copyright protect how an invention works?
items.correct: No — copyright protects original creative works such as drawings, text and music; the working principle of an invention can only be protected by a patent.
items.misconception: Writing your idea down and copyrighting it stops others manufacturing the product.
items.random_wrong: Copyright expires after one year unless renewed.
items.why: Students treat copyright as a catch-all protection; matching each form of IP to what it protects (function, appearance, brand, creative work) is a standard exam task.

items.q: Is a patent granted automatically when you invent something?
items.correct: No — you must apply, demonstrate the invention is new and inventive, and pay fees; protection lasts up to 20 years. Copyright is the protection that arises automatically.
items.misconception: Inventions are automatically patented the moment they are created.
items.random_wrong: Patents are issued by the shop that first sells the product.
items.why: Students confuse automatic copyright with the formal patent application process, missing the cost, scrutiny and time a patent involves.

items.q: Does a CE or UKCA mark mean a product was independently tested?
items.correct: Not necessarily — CE/UKCA is usually the manufacturer's own declaration of conformity with safety requirements; the BSI Kitemark is the mark showing independent testing.
items.misconception: The CE mark proves an independent laboratory tested the product.
items.random_wrong: CE stands for 'Customer Excellence' awarded by retailers.
items.why: Students assume all marks mean third-party testing; distinguishing self-declared conformity from independent certification is frequently examined.

items.random_wrong: The visual appearance of a product
items.why: Correct answer: How an invention works. “A brand name or logo” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What does the BSI Kitemark indicate?
items.correct: Independent testing against a British Standard
items.misconception: The product was made in Britain
items.random_wrong: The product is the cheapest available
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Independent testing against a British Standard. “The product was made in Britain” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015, goods must be what?
items.correct: Of satisfactory quality, fit for purpose and as described
items.misconception: Guaranteed for a minimum of ten years
items.why: Correct answer: Of satisfactory quality, fit for purpose and as described. “Guaranteed for a minimum of ten years” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Brand identifiers such as a name or logo
items.misconception: The working principle of an invention
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Brand identifiers such as a name or logo. “The working principle of an invention” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the maximum term of a UK patent, with renewal fees paid?
items.why: Exact answer: 20 years. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
